dailyJudgementSnippet: OpenAI won the day because both front-page Codex threads produced concrete user enthusiasm: commenters praised the new workflow and permissions UX, said Codex was already replacing some traditional CLI use, and shared successful reverse-engineering sessions rather than just reacting to a press release [E1][E2].
winnerExplanation: OpenAI had the strongest net signal because its coverage was concentrated in two substantial Codex stories with clear product-use praise and relatively limited backlash. The sentiment was driven by firsthand reports about useful terminal automation, slick interaction design, and successful hacking/debugging help, which made the positive reaction feel more grounded than a thin launch bump [E1][E2].

openai.judgementSnippet: Codex got the clearest user enthusiasm of the day: commenters praised the permissions and browser workflow, said it was already replacing some manual terminal work, and shared a successful TV/router reverse-engineering use case with only modest caveats about setup and source-code access [E1][E2].

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Claude drew the most discussion, but the biggest thread focused on confusing adaptive-thinking changes, stricter cyber refusals, oversubscription, and trust/communication complaints; the offsetting positives were mostly comparisons saying Claude already had features Codex is now adding and comments defending Opus on coding strength [E3][E4][E5][E6][E7].

google_gemini.judgementSnippet: Gemini's Mac app thread was pulled down by complaints about launch lag, limited model controls, required Google login, and ID-or-card age verification for CLI use, and a separate commenter contrasted fun demos with Gemini struggling on a basic slide-editing follow-up task [E8][E9].

evidence.summary: The 'Codex for almost everything' thread featured excitement about professional agents, praise for Codex's slick permissions flow and browser testing, and reports that it was already useful enough to replace some manual CLI work.

In 'Codex Hacked a Samsung TV', commenters shared strong firsthand success stories using Codex for reverse-engineering and router automation, even while noting that source-code access helped in this example.

The 'Claude Opus 4.7' launch thread was dominated by complaints about confusing adaptive thinking changes, stricter cybersecurity refusals, oversubscription, and reduced trust from recent model behavior.

The AI-cybersecurity debate thread treated Claude/Mythos claims skeptically, with comments arguing the setup was too closed to evaluate well and that Anthropic was benefiting from marketing around hard-to-verify capability claims.
Within the Codex discussion, commenters said Claude Desktop/Cowork and Claude Code already handled many of the same workflows, framing Anthropic as already ahead on some agent-product features.

Although the title claimed Qwen beat Claude Opus 4.7 on the pelican prompt, top comments pushed back and cited much stronger Opus coding performance, leaving a mixed signal rather than a clear knock on Claude.

The Claude Code token-usage tool reflected meaningful adoption but also cost/visibility pain, with one commenter citing $1400/week usage while another said the flat plan avoided rate limits in heavy use.

The 'Gemini app is now on Mac' thread criticized noticeable startup delay, minimal model-selection options, mandatory Google login, and age verification via government ID or credit card for CLI access.
In the Qwen-vs-Opus pelican thread, a commenter used Gemini as the contrast case, saying it struggled with a simple iterative diagram update and felt like a toy rather than a dependable tool.
